old_claim.note: Labor Unions & Collective Bargaining provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped system was created by a statutory accident insurance code, so formal legal-system machinery was required to establish institutions and claims rights.

source_shape.source_locator: Brief History: DGUV describes Bismarck ordering comprehensive social legislation and identifies the accident insurance code adopted on 6 July 1884 as the first of its kind worldwide.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: DGUV anchors the occupational accident insurance system to social legislation and an adopted accident insurance code.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A statutory insurance code requires formal legal-system machinery; the source does not identify labor unions or collective bargaining as the direct institutional substrate.

ontology_before: Occupational Accident Insurance depended directly on labor unions and collective bargaining, implying worker bargaining organization was the direct substrate for the 1884 statutory insurance system.
ontology_after: Occupational Accident Insurance depends on formal legal systems as the required statutory substrate for the accident insurance code and its claims institutions.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: occupational_accident_insurance now depends on legal_systems_common_civil and no longer directly depends on labor_unions_collective_bargaining.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to union-negotiated workplace benefit plans rather than statutory accident insurance.
A stronger source shows the 1884 German code was created by collective bargaining rather than state legislation.
A more precise modern_statutory_social_insurance_law node is added and replaces legal_systems_common_civil.
short_reason: The source-backed substrate is statutory law, not labor-union bargaining.
